title: OpenAI releases ChatGPT
domain_context: Consumer AI products compete primarily on interface accessibility and adoption velocity, so a chat-based product built on an existing transformer architecture was able to trigger industry-wide reallocation of R&D priorities around interface design and RLHF alignment rather than new core technology.
description: OpenAI released ChatGPT, a conversational interface built on its GPT-3.5 model trained using reinforcement learning from human feedback. The product reached 100 million users within two months of launch. Google issued an internal response reallocating engineering priorities, and ed-tech company Chegg reported a contraction in subscriber growth.

title: European Union passes the AI Act
domain_context: The AI Act applies extra-territorially and ties penalties to a company's global annual turnover, so any company wishing to offer AI products within the EU economic zone must comply with its risk-classification and documentation requirements regardless of where the company is headquartered.
description: The European Union passed the AI Act, establishing a legally binding, risk-based classification system for AI systems with financial penalties of up to 7% of a company's global annual turnover for non-compliance, plus documentation and copyright-transparency requirements for general-purpose AI models. Enforcement is phased in over several years.

title: Publishers file class action against Meta over AI training data
domain_context: Large language models are typically trained on massive text datasets obtained via web scraping or bulk downloads, and whether unlicensed copyrighted material can be used for this under "fair use" doctrine remains an unresolved legal question that this case directly tests.
description: Five publishers (Elsevier, Cengage, Hachette, Macmillan, and McGraw Hill) filed a class action against Meta and named CEO Mark Zuckerberg personally, alleging willful copyright infringement. Court discovery had previously shown Meta engineers downloading copyrighted books from shadow libraries to train AI models. An earlier, narrower 2023-2024 suit from 13 authors had been successfully defended by Meta.

title: Apple delays Apple Intelligence and later adopts external AI models
domain_context: Apple has historically pursued vertical integration by building its own hardware and software stack rather than relying on external competitors' technology for core product features.
description: Apple announced "Apple Intelligence," an on-device AI upgrade to Siri, at WWDC 2024, but the demonstrated features were not present in the shipped software and were delayed repeatedly. Internal reports described conflicting leadership views on approach, along with team restructuring in early 2025. By WWDC 2026, Apple rebuilt Siri using external AI models running on non-Apple hardware.

title: GT Advanced Technologies files for bankruptcy
domain_context: Apple's supplier contracts have historically included performance and yield-rate specifications that determine whether Apple is obligated to purchase the contracted materials, shifting manufacturing risk onto suppliers who must fund production capacity in advance of guaranteed orders.
description: Apple advanced GT Advanced Technologies $578 million to build furnaces for manufacturing synthetic sapphire intended for iPhone screens, under a contract that reportedly did not obligate Apple to purchase the material if it failed to meet specified yield rates. GT Advanced Technologies was unable to meet the required manufacturing specifications, exhausted its funds, and filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy. Apple reverted to its prior glass supplier shortly before mass production.
